q: Energy resources are all forms of fuel used in the modern world, either for heating, generation of electrical energy, or for other forms of energy ___ processes.
The different types of energy sources cause more or less ___ on the environment.
Renewable energy sources (non-conventional energy sources) do not pollute the environment and natural surrounding and require less ___.
In this chapter, we will learn about different sources of energy and their environmental ___.
Energy is the basic necessity for ___.
Energy demand is the term used to describe the ___ of energy by human activities.
Energy demand drives the whole energy system, influencing the total amount of ___ used.
Energy demand depends on different socioeconomic factors such as population, urbanization, industrialization, net capital income and development of ___.
Energy demand forecasting is crucial for ensuring future economic growth and environmental ___.
There has been an enormous increase in the global demand for energy in recent years as a result of industrial development and ___ growth.
Supply of energy is, therefore, far less than the actual ___.
The energy crisis is the concern that the world's demands on the ___ natural resources that are used to power industrial society are diminishing as the demand rises.
The energy crisis is a situation in which a nation suffers from a disruption of energy supplies connected by increasing energy prices that threaten economic and national ___.
With ___ percent of the world's oil reserves already discovered; people need to find new ways to make energy.
Since the non-renewable resources of the energy will not last long and cannot be regained, the world is going to face the problem of ___ in near future.
Renewable energy has huge potential to provide solution to increase energy crisis and it is the key factor to the future of energy, food and economic ___.
In our daily life we use different forms of energy such as heat energy, light energy, mechanical energy, electrical energy, chemical energy and ___ energy.
The most common forms of energy are heat, light and ___.
All the sources of energy can be divided into two categories: renewable sources and ___ sources of energy.
A non-renewable resource is a natural substance that is not replenished with the ___ at which it is consumed.
A non-renewable resource is a ___ resource.
Non-renewable energy resources include coal, natural gas, crude oil, and ___ energy.
Non-renewable energy resources occur in limited and ___ quantities.
Non-renewable energy resources cannot be regenerated in a short period of time or used again and ___.
Renewable energy sources (also called non-conventional energy sources) are continuously replenished by ___ processes.
Renewable energy is the energy generated by using wind, solar, hydro, ___, etc.
Renewable energy sources cannot be exhausted easily and can be generated at a ___ rate for their use over and over again.
Non-renewable energy sources are available only in ___ supply.
Fossil fuels (like coal, oil, natural gases, etc.) and nuclear energy are examples of ___ energy sources.
It takes a long time (often millions of years) for ___ resources to be replenished.
Renewable resources are replenished naturally and over ___ periods of time.
Solar energy, hydro energy, wind energy and geothermal energy are examples of ___ resources.
Fossil fuels, such as coal, oil and natural gas, are important ___ sources of energy.
___ is the main constituent of these fossil fuels.
A large amount of ___ energy is stored in the fossil fuels.
This stored chemical energy in fossil fuels is converted into various other forms of energy such as heat, light and ___ energy.
Fossil fuels are excellent sources of energy for our ___ needs.
The fossil fuel industry extracts ___ from the ground and converts it to gasoline.
Fossil fuel liquids are refined into ___ products that are used as ingredients in the manufacture of plastics and polyurethane to solvents.
Coal is formed through a process called ___.
Coal is made of decomposed plant matter in conditions of high temperature and ___.
Coal is not a uniform substance; its composition varies from ___ to deposit.
___ is another major source of the energy in our country.
Natural gas (methane) does not take nearly as long to ___.
Methane is a common gas found in ___ and is also the by-product of animals' digestive system.
Nuclear energy is ___ as the uranium fuels used are consumed in the nuclear fission reaction.
Energy produced in a ___ can be harnessed to produce electricity.
Nuclear energy is also being used to power submarines and ___.
Vessels driven by nuclear energy can sail for long periods without having to ___.
___ obtained as by-products in nuclear reactions are used in medicine, agriculture and research.
There are several alternative and renewable sources of energy which are not only environment friendly but can also be available in ___.
The major types of renewable energy sources are: (i) solar, (ii) wind, (iii) hydropower, (iv) ocean, (v) geothermal and (vi) ___.
___ is one of the most powerful renewable sources of energy.
Solar energy is the ___ light and heat from the sun that has been harnessed by humans since ancient times using a range of ever-evolving technologies.
Earth's atmosphere and clouds absorb or scatter as much as ___% of the incoming sunlight.
The sunlight that reaches the ground consists of visible light, infrared, ultraviolet and other forms of ___ radiation.
The total amount of radiation energy passing through per unit area and per unit time is called ___.
Irradiance is measured in $W\ m^{-2}$ or ___ $m^{-2}$.
Another commonly used term for irradiance of sunlight is ___.
Insolation is the solar irradiance received on a ___ surface such as ground.
Above the earth's atmosphere, solar radiation has an intensity of approximately ___ $W\ m^{-2}$.
This value is known as the solar ___.
At the earth's surface solar radiation has an intensity of approximately ___ $W\ m^{-2}$ on a clear day at solar noon in the summer months.
There are three main ways to harness solar energy: photovoltaic solar cell, solar heating, and concentrated solar ___ (CSP).
Conversion of light energy into electrical energy is based on a phenomenon called ___ effect.
When semiconductor materials are exposed to light, some of the ___ of light are absorbed by the semiconductor crystal.
Absorption of photons in semiconductor crystal causes a significant number of free ___ in the crystal.
A solar cell (also known as a photovoltaic cell) is defined as an electrical device that converts ___ energy into electrical energy through the photovoltaic effect.
A solar cell is basically a ___ diode.
Light energy, in the form of photons, supplies sufficient energy to the p-n junction to create a number of ___ pairs.
Individual solar cells can be combined to form modules commonly known as solar ___.
When combined into a large solar panel, considerable amounts of ___ energy can be generated.
Achieving the perfect perpendicular sun rays is the ___ orientation so that solar panels receive direct light throughout the day.
Power conversion efficiency of the solar panel is the ratio of the electrical power delivered by the panel to the ___ power received by it.
Solar heating can also play an important role in reducing the impact of global ___.
Solar water heating systems installed in homes are comprised of three main elements: the solar collector, insulated piping, and a hot water ___ tank.
The solar collector gathers the ___ from solar radiation and transfers it to water.
___ collectors are the most common type of collector.
Flat plate collectors have copper pipes attached to an absorber plate contained in an insulated box that is covered with a tempered glass or ___ cover plate.
Concentrated solar power (CSP) systems generate solar power by using mirrors or ___ to concentrate a large area of sunlight.
Concentrated solar power (CSP) systems concentrate sunlight onto a specific point of ___ which converts into heat.
The heat generated by CSP systems is then used to create ___, which drives a turbine to generate electrical power.
CSP plants consist of two parts: one that collects solar energy and converts it to heat, and another that converts the heat energy to ___.
There are four types of CSP technologies: (a) Parabolic trough systems, (b) Linear fresnel systems, (c) Power tower systems and (d) Parabolic ___ systems.
Wind energy is the energy generated or produced by harnessing the power of the ___.
Like solar power, harnessing the wind is highly dependent on weather and ___.
Wind energy is one of the oldest and cleanest forms of energy and the most developed of the ___ energy sources.
Both windmills and ___ harness wind energy and put it to practical use.
Windmills convert wind energy directly into ___ energy for such tasks as milling grain and pumping water.
A wind turbine converts wind energy into ___.
In a wind turbine, wind first hits the blades of the turbine, causing them to ___ and turn the turbine connected to them.
The rotation changes kinetic energy to ___ energy, by rotating a shaft which is connected to a generator.
Electrical energy is produced through ___ in a wind turbine.
Wind farms consist of many individual wind turbines, which are connected to the electric power ___ network.
The amount of power that can be harvested from wind depends on the size of the turbine and the ___ of its blades.
Wind energy is the kinetic energy of ___ in motion.
In the wind power formula, $\rho$ is the density of ___.
The output is proportional to the dimensions of the rotor and to the ___ of the wind speed.
Theoretically, when wind speed doubles, wind power potential increases by a factor of ___.
Wind turbines are mounted on a ___ to capture the most energy.
At 100 feet (30 meters) or more above ground, wind turbines can take advantage of the faster and less ___ wind.
Turbines catch the wind energy with their ___ blades.
Usually, two or ___ blades are mounted on a shaft to form a rotor.
The flowing water and water stored in huge dams is also a very important source of energy which is known as ___ energy.
Hydropower, or hydroelectric power, is one of the oldest and largest sources of ___ energy.
The source of power in a hydroelectric plant is the ___ of water.
Hydroelectric energy is an ___ source of electricity that costs less than most of other sources of energy.
However, overdevelopment and unrestricted harnessing of water power can have a devastating effect on the local environment and ___ areas.
There are three types of hydropower facilities: impoundment, diversion, and ___ storage.
The most common type of hydroelectric power plant is an ___ facility.
An impoundment facility typically uses a ___ to store river water in a reservoir.
Water is collected or stored at a higher elevation and ___ pulls the water downward through large pipes or tunnels (penstocks) to a lower elevation.
The difference in elevation between where water is stored and where it falls to in a hydropower plant is known as the ___.
At the end of its passage down the pipes, the falling water causes ___ to rotate.
The turbines in turn drive generators, which convert mechanical energy into ___.
___ are used to convert the alternating voltage of the generators to a higher voltage suitable for long-distance transmission.
A penstock is a closed conduit that channels the flow of water to turbines with water flow regulated by ___ and valves.
A diversion, sometimes called a ___ facility, channels a portion of a river through a canal or a penstock.
A diversion facility utilizes the natural decline of the river bed elevation to produce ___.
A diversion may not require the use of a ___.
Another type of hydropower is ___ storage hydropower (PSH).
A PSH facility is able to ___ the electricity generated by other power sources.
When the demand for electricity is low, a PSH facility stores energy by pumping water from the ___ reservoir to an upper reservoir at a higher elevation.
During periods of high electrical demand, the water is released back to the lower reservoir and turns a turbine, generating ___.
A hydropower resource can be evaluated by its available ___.
The power available from falling water can be calculated from the flow rate and density of water, the height of fall, and the local acceleration due to ___.
In the hydroelectric power formula, $P$ is useful power output measured in ___.
In the hydroelectric power formula, $\eta$ is efficiency of the turbine and is ___.
In the hydroelectric power formula, $\rho$ is ___ of water.
In the hydroelectric power formula, $h$ is difference in ___ between the outlet and inlet.
Volumetric flow rate $V = \frac{V}{t}$ is measured in ___ $s^{-1}$.
Most conventional hydroelectric plants include a ___ that raises the water level of the river and controls flow of water.
A water turbine converts the kinetic energy of falling water into ___ energy.
A ___ is connected to the turbine by shafts and converts the mechanical energy from the turbine to electrical energy.
The ___ system transports electricity from the hydropower station to consumers.
Due to the gravitational pull of mainly the ___ on the spinning earth, the level of water in the sea rises and falls.
This phenomenon is called high and low ___.
Tidal energy is produced by the ___ in sea levels.
Tidal energy is harnessed by constructing a dam (a ___ barrage) across a narrow opening to the sea.
A tidal barrage works similarly to a large hydropower reservoir dam but it is placed at the entrance to a ___ or estuary.
A turbine fixed at the opening of the tidal barrage converts tidal energy to ___.
Similar to river current technologies, turbines anchored to the ocean floor or suspended from a buoy in the path of an ocean ___ could also be used to generate power.
The ___ energy of huge waves near the seashore can be trapped to generate electricity.
The waves are generated by strong ___ blowing across the sea.
Most wave energy systems capture energy on the ___ of waves or use pressure differences just below the surface.
The water at the surface of the sea or ocean is heated by the ___ while the water in deeper sections is relatively cold.
This difference in temperature is exploited to obtain energy in ocean-thermal-energy ___ plants.
These plants can operate if the temperature difference between the water at the surface and water at depths up to 2 km is ___ $K\ (20\ °C)$ or more.
The warm surface-water is used to boil a ___ liquid (e.g. ammonia, boiling point $\sim -33.34\ °C$).
The vapours of the liquid (ammonia) are then used to run the ___ of generator.
The cold water from the depth of the ocean is pumped up and condense ___ again to liquid.
The energy potential from the sea (tidal energy, wave energy and ocean thermal energy) is quite large, but efficient commercial exploitation is ___.
Geothermal energy is a type of renewable energy taken from the Earth's ___ (the internal heat of the earth).
Geothermal energy is one of the oldest types of ___ sources of heat.
However, geothermal energy is limited to regions of the world where there are hot ___ rocks.
Thermal energy from ___ can be used to generate electricity.
___ is extremely hot liquid and semi-liquid rock located under Earth's surface.
Wells of up to a ___ deep or more are drilled into underground reservoirs to tap into the geothermal resources.
These resources can be exploited from naturally occurring heat, rock and water ___.
Cooled water is pumped into heated rocks where it is turned into ___, which then rises up to drive the turbines and generate electricity.
Condensed water in geothermal energy production is ___ and the process is repeated.
Biomass is renewable ___ material that comes from plants and animals.
Biomass contains stored ___ energy.
Wood and wood processing wastes (e.g. firewood, furniture mill sawdust and waste from pulp and paper mills) are sources of ___.
Agricultural crops and waste materials (e.g. corn, soybeans, sugar cane, woody plants, algae, rice husk and food processing residues) are sources of ___.
Biogenic materials in municipal solid waste (e.g. paper, cotton, wool products, food and wood wastes) are sources of ___.
Animal manure and human ___ are sources of biomass.

Biomass is converted to energy through various processes, including: direct ___ (burning) to produce heat, thermochemical conversion to produce solid, gaseous, and liquid fuels, chemical conversion to produce liquid fuels, biological conversion to produce liquid and gaseous fuels.
___ is the most common method for converting biomass to useful energy.
All biomass can be burned directly for heating buildings and water, for industrial heat process, and for generating electricity in ___ turbines.
Thermochemical conversion of biomass includes ___ and gasification.
Pyrolysis and gasification are thermal ___ processes.
In thermochemical conversion, biomass feedstock materials are heated in closed, pressurized vessels called ___ at high temperatures.
Biomass pyrolysis produces fuels such as charcoal, bio-oil, renewable diesel, methane, and ___.
Biomass gasification produces a carbon monoxide and hydrogen rich gas called ___ gas or syngas.
Syngas can be used as a fuel for diesel engines, for heating, and for generating electricity in ___ turbines.
Chemical conversion process known as ___ is used for converting vegetable oils, animal fats, and greases into biodiesel.
Biological conversion includes ___ to convert biomass into ethanol.
Biological conversion includes anaerobic ___ to produce natural gas.
Ethanol is used as a ___ fuel.
Natural gas, also called ___ or biomethane, is produced in anaerobic digesters at sewage treatment plants and at dairy and livestock operations.
Properly treated renewable natural gas has the same uses as ___ natural gas.
Apart from the problem of energy resources getting depleted, energy harnessing and utilization cause an immense amount of environmental ___.
The environmental problems directly related to energy production and consumption include air pollution, climate change, water pollution, thermal pollution, and solid waste ___.
It is important to understand the environmental impacts associated with producing electrical power from both non-renewable and ___ sources.

The problems that relate to the use of fossil fuels are: global warming, acid rains, dangers posed by leaded fuels, oil spills, gas leaks and explosions, air pollution and ___ pollution caused by poorly managed coal mines.
___ refers to the gradual increase in the average temperature of the earth's surface and its atmosphere which has been attributed to the accumulation of greenhouse gases.
Global warming has been attributed to the accumulation of ___ gases.
The main greenhouse gases are carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$), methane ($\text{CH}_4$), water vapour, oxides of nitrogen ($\text{NO}_x$) and ___.
___ also destroy the earth's protective ozone layer which shields the earth from harmful ultraviolet (UV) rays from the sun.
Acid rains are caused mainly by the release of ___ ($\text{SO}_2$) and oxides of nitrogen ($\text{NO}_x$) when fossil fuels burn.
The oxides combine with water vapour in the air to form acids, which return to the ground as ___ rain.
Because ___ acid is a relatively weak acid, the ability of carbon dioxide alone to generate true acid rain is very limited.
Acidified clouds could travel great distances before releasing the ___ rain.
The problems posed by acid rains include corrosion of the built environment, soil degradation, ___ pollution and depletion of forests.
The oil industry adds ___ (Pb) to petrol (gasoline) to help engines run more smoothly.
Vehicles that burn leaded gasoline pour out leaded fumes that contaminate the ___.
The World Health Organisation (WHO) has established that smoke from the combustion of leaded fuels in vehicles causes cancer and high ___ in adults.
In children, smoke from leaded fuels impairs mental development, reduces intelligence thus hindering learning ability and causes ___ disorders.
The leakage of fuel oil from storage vessels, oil tankers, pipelines and tanker trucks is ___.
Oil spills seriously damage the land, vegetation, and ___ bodies, including the oceans.
In addition, spilled oil can damage the ecosystem and its ___.
___ leaks and explosions sometimes accompany the harnessing and utilization of fossil fuels especially in the coal mines and storage plants.
Excavated areas that have been ___ for coal but not filled and revegetated cause water pollution.
Surface water runoff from mined areas can flush sediments and ___ compounds into nearby streams and rivers.
Emissions from vehicles, thermal power plants and factories contain unburned hydrocarbons, particulates, carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide and ___ of nitrogen and sulphur.
These substances in the air could irritate the eyes, ___ and the lungs.
___ is now a proven technology which is inherently safe, as opposed to some dangerous electricity generating technologies.
A solar module will prevent the emission of ___ produced by other conventional energy sources.
Photovoltaic systems make no ___ and cause no pollution while in operation.
PV cell technologies have relatively lower environmental risks compared to other types of ___ sources.
Chemicals used in PV cells in the manufacturing facility, the installation site, and the disposal or recycling facility could be released to air, surface water, and ___.
A wind farm, when installed on agricultural land, has one of the ___ environmental impacts of all energy sources.
A wind farm occupies less land area per ___ (kWh) of electricity generated than any other energy conversion system.
Greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution produced by its construction are very ___ and declining.
Modern wind turbine designs have significantly reduced the ___ from turbines.
Low frequency sound and ___ (i.e. usually beneath the threshold of human hearing) are everywhere in the environment.
Modern turbine designs which locate the blades ___ instead of downwind have significantly reduced the level of infrasound.
Wind turbines may create ___ on nearby residences when the sun passes behind the turbine.
Shadow flicker can be avoided by locating the wind farm to avoid unacceptable shadow flicker, or turning the turbine ___ for a few minutes.
Although hydropower has no air ___ impacts, construction and operation of hydropower dams can significantly affect natural river systems as well as fish and wildlife populations.
Construction and operation of hydropower dams can significantly affect natural river systems as well as fish and ___ populations.
Hydropower has major effect on river transportation, agriculture, fertile lands, ___.
Assessment of the environmental impacts of a specific hydropower facility requires ___ review.
In flat basins large dams cause ___ of large tracts of land, destroying local animals and habitats.
Flooding caused by large dams destroys local ___ and habitats.
Villages and people have to be ___ causing change in life style due to dam construction.
Large amounts of plant life are submerged and decay ___ due to dam construction.
Dams affect the ___ pattern of river animals.
Dams restrict ___ that are responsible for the fertile lands downstream.
Large dams are breeding grounds for ___ and cause the spread of disease.
Dams serve as a heat sink, water in dams is ___ than the normal river water.
When warm water from dams is released into the river downstream, it can affect ___ life.
Geothermal power is a relatively ___ (less harmful) source of energy.
For the most part, the impacts of geothermal development are ___.
Electricity generation from geothermal resources involves much lower greenhouse gas ___ rates than that from fossil fuels.
However, geothermal development could have certain negative impacts if appropriate ___ actions and monitoring plants are not in place.
Any large-scale construction and drilling operation in geothermal energy will produce visual impacts on the landscape, create noise and ___ and affect local economies.
Biomass power plants share some similarities with ___ power plants.
Both biomass and fossil fuel power plants involve the ___ of a feedstock to generate electricity.
Biomass plants raise similar, but not identical, concerns about air emissions and water use similar to ___ plants.
Biomass power plants, like coal and natural gas-fired power plants, require water for ___.
Land use impacts from biomass power production are driven primarily by the type of ___ used.
Global warming emissions are associated with growing and harvesting biomass feedstock, transporting feedstock to the power plant, and burning or ___ the feedstock.
Transportation and combustion emissions are roughly equivalent for all types of ___.
Global warming emissions from the sourcing of biomass feedstock vary ___.
For fossil fuels, chemical energy converts to thermal energy, then to kinetic energy, and finally to ___ energy.
For solar energy, light & thermal energy converts to ___ energy.
For nuclear energy, nuclear energy converts to thermal energy, then to kinetic energy, and finally to ___ energy.
For wind energy, ___ energy converts to electrical energy.
For bio-fuel, chemical energy converts to thermal energy, then to mechanical energy, and finally to ___ energy.
For hydroelectricity, ___ potential energy converts to kinetic energy, then to electrical energy.
For geothermal energy, thermal energy converts to kinetic energy, then to ___ energy.
For tidal energy, ___ energy converts to electrical energy.
